question: In the particle model of matter, which statement correctly compares a liquid with a gas of the same substance at the same temperature?

options.A: Particles in the liquid have no kinetic energy; particles in the gas do.
options.B: Particles in the liquid are typically closer together than particles in the gas.
options.C: Particles in the liquid move only by vibration about fixed positions.
options.D: Particles in the gas have stronger intermolecular forces than in the liquid.
options.E: The average kinetic energy of particles is higher in the liquid than in the gas.

hint: Compare spacing and freedom of movement, not temperature.

steps.title: Recall the particle model
steps.content: In a liquid, particles are close (touching neighbours) but can move past each other. In a gas at the same temperature, particles are much farther apart on average.

steps.title: Eliminate distractors
steps.content: Average kinetic energy depends on temperature, so it is the same for liquid and gas at the same temperature. Liquids are not fixed-lattice solids. Intermolecular forces are stronger in liquids than gases.

question: Brownian motion of smoke particles in air is best explained by:

options.A: smoke particles being electrically charged and repelling each other
options.B: uneven bombardment of smoke particles by fast-moving air molecules
options.C: convection currents caused only by temperature differences in the room
options.D: gravity pulling denser smoke particles downward in zig-zag paths
options.E: smoke particles absorbing light and heating unevenly

hint: Think about collisions with invisible air molecules.

steps.title: Link observation to molecular collisions
steps.content: Smoke particles are large enough to see but small enough that random collisions with air molecules are unbalanced at any instant, producing a jittery path.

steps.title: Why not the alternatives
steps.content: Charge, convection, gravity, and heating can move particles, but they do not explain the characteristic random zig-zag of Brownian motion.

question: According to the kinetic theory of gases, gas pressure on a container wall arises mainly from:

options.A: attractive forces between gas particles and the wall
options.B: particles colliding with the wall and changing momentum
options.C: particles vibrating within a fixed crystal lattice
options.D: electrons flowing from the gas into the wall
options.E: the weight of the gas resting on the bottom of the container only

hint: Pressure is force per area — what force do particles exert on the wall?

steps.title: Momentum change at the wall
steps.content: Each collision with the wall changes the particle's momentum; the rate of momentum change is a force. Averaged over many collisions, this is the gas pressure.

steps.title: Check the claim
steps.content: Attraction, lattice vibration, electron flow, and weight-only pictures do not match the kinetic-theory account of pressure throughout the gas.

question: Which gas makes up the largest percentage by volume of dry air?
hint: Recall the approximate percentages of the main gases in air.

steps.title: Recall dry-air composition
steps.content: Dry air is roughly 78% nitrogen, 21% oxygen, ~1% argon, and a small fraction of carbon dioxide and other gases.

steps.title: Select the majority component
steps.content: Nitrogen is by far the largest component by volume.

question: How many millimetres are there in $2.5$ metres?
hint: Convert metres to millimetres using powers of ten.

steps.title: Check scale
steps.content: A few metres should be thousands of millimetres, not tens or hundred-thousands.
